papers.title: Deep learning model based on MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for noninvasive assessment of lymphovascular invasion in rectal cancer patients.
papers.abstract: Accurate preoperative assessment of lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in patients with rectal cancer (RC) is important for guiding postoperative management. This study aimed to develop and validate a deep learning model based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for the preoperative assessment of LVI in RC patients.
papers.summary: This study developed a deep learning model that uses MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters to noninvasively assess lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in rectal cancer patients before surgery. The model aims to improve preoperative decision-making by accurately predicting LVI status, which is critical for guiding postoperative management.

papers.title: Adaptive Self-Attention Graph Pooling for Drug-Target Affinity Prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-target affinity (DTA) prediction is a critical step in drug discovery and precision medicine. Although graph neural networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable progress, existing graph pooling methods rely on fixed ratios, failing to adapt to the structural diversity of molecules and proteins, which leads to information loss or redundant feature retention. To address this issue, we propose the Adaptive Self-Attention Graph Pooling (ASAGPooling) mechanism, which introduces a learnable pooling ratio that dynamically adjusts node retention during training. Furthermore, we develop ASAG-DTA, a mu
papers.summary: This paper introduces ASAGPooling, a new graph pooling method that adaptively adjusts node retention during training to better handle the structural diversity of molecules and proteins in drug-target affinity prediction. The authors develop ASAG-DTA, which improves prediction accuracy by avoiding information loss or redundancy caused by fixed pooling ratios.

papers.title: MD-Transformer: Multimodal Integration of ProtBERT Embeddings and Physicochemical Descriptors for Protein-Protein Interface Residue Prediction.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-protein interaction (PPI) interface residues is essential for understanding molecular recognition and supporting structure-guided design. To integrate contextual sequence representations with structure-related physicochemical information, we propose a multimodal framework termed MD-Transformer. The model combines residue-level ProtBERT embeddings with physicochemical descriptors, including B-factor, solvent-accessible surface area (SASA), and hydrophobicity. A hybrid fusion module first aligns heterogeneous features, followed by Transformer encoding and cross-mod
papers.summary: This paper introduces MD-Transformer, a multimodal deep learning model that combines ProtBERT sequence embeddings with physicochemical features (B-factor, SASA, hydrophobicity) to predict protein-protein interaction interface residues. The model achieves improved accuracy by fusing contextual sequence information with structure-related descriptors, demonstrating the value of integrating multiple data modalities for this prediction task.

papers.title: PRED-TMSdeep: Prediction of Transmembrane Topology and Signal Peptides Using Deep Learning.
papers.abstract: Accurate annotation of secreted and membrane proteins requires detecting N-terminal secretion signals, locating their cleavage sites, and distinguishing secretion-signal classes, while also predicting full transmembrane topology for both alpha-helical and beta-barrel proteins. Current tools typically address either whole-protein topology with a generic signal-peptide category or signal-peptide type classification without integrated topology annotation, leaving end-to-end labels incomplete when both features must be resolved together. Here, we present PRED-TMSdeep, a deep learning method that j
papers.summary: PRED-TMSdeep is a new deep learning tool that simultaneously predicts transmembrane topology and identifies signal peptides in proteins, addressing a gap where existing methods handle these tasks separately. It can handle both alpha-helical and beta-barrel membrane proteins, providing more complete end-to-end annotation for secreted and membrane proteins.

papers.title: Training PBertKla on an Integrated Multi-Source Dataset with a Machine-Learning Layer for Lysine Lactylation Site Prediction.
papers.abstract: Lysine lactylation (Kla) is a recently discovered post-translational modification implicated in energy metabolism, cellular reprogramming, and disease progression. Here, we train the existing ProteinBERT-based predictor PBertKla on an integrated multi-source dataset and augment it with a lightweight machine-learning (ML) layer over sequence-derived features to predict Kla sites; on a common blind test set, the resulting model (PBertKla + ML) reaches an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.9126 on the integrated set and is statistically indistinguishable from the
papers.summary: This paper improves prediction of lysine lactylation sites by training the ProteinBERT-based model PBertKla on a combined multi-source dataset and adding a lightweight machine-learning layer. The enhanced model achieves high accuracy (AUROC 0.9126) on blind testing, offering a useful tool for studying this post-translational modification.

papers.title: Composition Descriptors and Cultivar Transferability in Machine-Learning Models of Ultrasonication-Induced Functional Properties of Rice Flour.
papers.abstract: Flow-cell ultrasonication of gelatinized rice flour slurries alters cultivar-dependent water solubility, viscosity, and retrogradation of pregelatinized rice flour, properties important for plant-based beverages and convenience foods. We tested whether cultivar-level composition descriptors, amylose, protein, and fiber, can represent cultivar-associated variation in ultrasonication responses while separating process-only prediction, within-domain cultivar representation, and unseen-cultivar transfer. Six rice cultivars were processed across nine amplitude-time combinations and two slurry conce
papers.summary: This study investigates how machine learning models can predict the functional properties of ultrasonicated rice flour based on cultivar composition (amylose, protein, fiber), finding that these descriptors enable reasonable prediction even for unseen cultivars. The work demonstrates that cultivar-level composition data can represent variation in processing responses, which is useful for developing plant-based food ingredients.

papers.title: A surrogate-assisted framework for the subject-specific scalable design of compliant prosthetic wrists.
papers.abstract: This study presents a surrogate-assisted computational framework for the scalable design of compliant prosthetic wrist joints. The framework integrates computer-aided design (CAD) parameterization, automated finite element analysis (FEA), machine-learning-based surrogate modeling, and multi-objective optimization. It was applied to a compliant rolling-contact joint (CRCJ)-based prosthetic wrist capable of flexion-extension and radial-ulnar deviation. Range of motion (RoM), initial stiffness, and final stiffness were defined as design objectives to achieve biomechanically relevant wrist behavio
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational framework that uses machine learning to optimize the design of compliant prosthetic wrists for individual patients. The framework combines CAD, finite element analysis, and multi-objective optimization to achieve biomechanically relevant wrist behavior.

papers.title: Calibration-aware genomic prediction of paratuberculosis serostatus in indigenous Turkish goats.
papers.abstract: Paratuberculosis, caused by Mycobacterium avium subsp. paratuberculosis (MAP), is a chronic, incurable enteritis of ruminants in which late-onset clinical signs preclude timely intervention, making early genomic stratification a key control lever. We investigated the genetic architecture underlying the host antibody response to MAP - measured as anti-MAP ELISA serostatus - using genome-wide SNP data from 474 animals representing seven indigenous Turkish goat breeds sampled across 11 provinces (237 seropositive, 237 seronegative; estimated true seroprevalence 14.1%). Fourteen architecturally di
papers.summary: This study uses genome-wide SNP data from 474 indigenous Turkish goats to investigate the genetic basis of antibody response to paratuberculosis, a chronic ruminant disease. The authors identify 14 genetic variants associated with serostatus and develop genomic prediction models, finding that calibration-aware approaches improve prediction accuracy. This work provides a foundation for early genetic stratification to aid disease control in goat populations.

papers.title: AlphaGEM enables precise genome-scale metabolic modelling by integrating protein structure alignment with deep-learning-based dark metabolism mining.
papers.abstract: Constructing high-quality genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) for non-model organisms remains challenging. To address this, we developed AlphaGEM, a versatile toolbox leveraging proteome-scale structural alignment, protein language models (PLMSearch), and deep-learning-based predictions for efficient genomic mining to generate GEMs ready for applications. AlphaGEM enhances homologous relationship identification compared to traditional sequence-based methods. Crucially, it employs an ensemble procedure empowered by multiple deep learning toolboxes to effectively mine dark metabolic functions e
papers.summary: AlphaGEM is a new computational toolbox that uses protein structure alignment and deep learning to build high-quality genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) for non-model organisms. It improves the identification of homologous relationships and can uncover previously hidden metabolic functions, making GEM construction more accurate and applicable.

papers.title: A cell-based kinetic framework enables TCR specificity prediction.
papers.abstract: The ability to predict T-cell receptor (TCR) specificity from sequences could transform immunotherapy, vaccine development, and our understanding of immune recognition. However, progress has been shaped by "edge cases", in which specificity appears to be captured by simplified descriptors, such as sequence motifs or correlations between binding affinity and functional activation. Although informative, these regimes are not representative of the general mode of TCR recognition. Emphasizing such cases has contributed to a drift in the field, where both experimental assay design and computational
papers.summary: This paper introduces a cell-based kinetic framework that improves prediction of T-cell receptor (TCR) specificity from sequence data, addressing limitations of simplified descriptors like sequence motifs or binding affinity correlations. The framework captures more general modes of TCR recognition, potentially advancing immunotherapy and vaccine development.

papers.title: Wiz regulates clustered protocadherin genes by restricting CTCF/cohesin loop extrusion in a genomic-distance biased manner.
papers.abstract: Zinc finger proteins (ZFPs or ZNFs) constitute the largest family of transcription factors in mammals; however, their regulatory mechanism remains largely elusive. Here we propose COP (C2H2-ZFP occupancy predictor), a deep learning-based heuristic screening tool that integrates DNA sequence with protein primary and secondary features to assess ZFP genomic enrichments. Applying COP to the mouse clustered protocadherin (cPcdh) gene locus, we identified dozens of C2H2-ZFPs potentially involved in CTCF-mediated gene regulation with Wiz (widely interspaced zinc finger-containing protein) having the
papers.summary: This study introduces COP, a deep learning tool that predicts where C2H2 zinc finger proteins bind to DNA by integrating sequence and protein features. Applying COP to the clustered protocadherin locus identified Wiz as a key regulator that restricts CTCF/cohesin loop extrusion in a genomic-distance biased manner. The work provides a new computational approach for studying the largest family of mammalian transcription factors.

papers.title: Specificity-driven cell-gene graph learning identifies rare cell states in single-cell and spatial transcriptomic data.
papers.abstract: Detecting rare cell populations that drive development, differentiation, and disease-associated transformation remains a central challenge in biology and medicine. Although these populations often represent promising targets for intervention, they are difficult to resolve from single-cell transcriptomic data because most methods rely on homophily-based cell-cell similarity, which can merge rare cells into dominant populations and mask their subtle transcriptional signatures. The challenge is further amplified in multi-sample analyses, where batch correction can dilute rare-cell-specific signal
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational method that uses specificity-driven cell-gene graph learning to identify rare cell states from single-cell and spatial transcriptomic data. Unlike traditional approaches that rely on cell-cell similarity and can miss rare populations, this method preserves subtle transcriptional signatures that might otherwise be lost during batch correction or merged into larger cell groups. The approach addresses a key challenge in detecting rare but biologically important cell types involved in development, differentiation, and disease.

papers.title: Evaluation of deep learning architectures for predicting ligand interactions with neurologically relevant GPCRs.
papers.abstract: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) are therapeutic targets for over 30% of approved drugs, yet specific GPCR subtypes act as molecular initiating events in several neurotoxic adverse outcome pathways. Therefore, knowledge of GPCR-ligand interactions is critical for drug discovery and computational toxicology. However, accurate predictions of GPCR-ligand binding can be challenging due to receptor conformational flexibility, complex membrane environment, and lack of selectivity among ligands. Drug-target interaction (DTI) models that jointly encode protein and ligand representations offer a pro
papers.summary: This study evaluates various deep learning architectures for predicting how ligands interact with neurologically relevant GPCRs, a key class of drug targets. The authors compare different models that jointly encode protein and ligand information, aiming to improve accuracy in predicting GPCR-ligand binding, which is challenging due to receptor flexibility and ligand selectivity issues. The findings provide guidance on selecting effective deep learning approaches for drug discovery and computational toxicology applications.

papers.title: OmiXAI: An ensemble XAI pipeline for interpretable deep learning in omics data.
papers.abstract: Deep learning methods have become methods of choice in the analysis of genomic data. The performance of deep learning models depends on the information available for training. A growing trend in deep learning applications involves leveraging multi-omics data - spanning genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and other domains. When a deep learning model trained on omics data achieves high performance, the important question is to define factors that contribute to model's predictive power. Explainable AI (XAI) methods can be categorized as model-aware and model-agnosti
papers.summary: OmiXAI is a new ensemble pipeline that integrates multiple explainable AI methods to interpret deep learning models trained on multi-omics data. It helps researchers identify which biological features drive model predictions, addressing a key challenge in applying deep learning to genomics and other omics fields.

papers.title: Popformer: Learning general signatures of positive selection with a self-supervised transformer.
papers.abstract: Understanding natural selection can help shed light on the genetics underpinning adaptive evolution. The widespread availability of large-scale human genetic variation data has led to the development of data-driven methods for detecting signatures of selection, many of which are based on deep learning. However, these methods often fail to generalize well to the diversity of selection signatures across a broad range of evolutionary scenarios. We propose a novel transformer-based model, Popformer, for learning encodings of general patterns of genetic variation. Popformer includes site-wise and h
papers.summary: This paper introduces Popformer, a transformer-based deep learning model that learns general signatures of positive selection from genetic variation data. Unlike previous methods that struggle to generalize across diverse evolutionary scenarios, Popformer uses self-supervised learning to capture broad patterns of selection, improving detection of adaptive evolution in human genetic data.

papers.title: Construction and validation of a phenotypic prediction model for bacterial gentamicin resistance using deep learning with gene sequences.
papers.abstract: The emergence of bacterial resistance to antibiotics poses a significant threat to human health; thus, there is an urgent need for new strategies in understanding the mechanisms of resistance and further fast prediction of it. Deep learning models offer promising solutions through analyzing genetic sequences in the prediction of bacterial resistance patterns. This study develops and validates a transformer-based deep learning model, DNABERT-2-117M, to predict gentamicin resistance in Klebsiella pneumoniae directly from whole-genome sequences. Our central methodological advance investigates the
papers.summary: This study developed a transformer-based deep learning model (DNABERT-2-117M) to predict gentamicin resistance in Klebsiella pneumoniae directly from whole-genome sequences. The model offers a promising approach for fast prediction of antibiotic resistance patterns, potentially aiding clinical decision-making and surveillance efforts.

papers.title: Prediction of axillary lymph node metastasis using a transformer model and multi-omics validation in breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Our study developed a multiomics-driven transformer model that combines mammography, MRI, transcriptomic and proteomic data to noninvasively predict axillary lymph node (ALN) metastasis in breast cancer. A total of 2105 patients from 10 institutions were included for model training and validation. The model achieved an AUC of 0.939 in the training cohort (n = 658) and 0.830-0.867 across three independent validation cohorts (n = 282, 971 and 194, respectively), outperforming conventional ultrasound examination. Grad-CAM visualizations highlighted the tumor edges and surrounding tissue, consiste
papers.summary: This study developed a transformer model integrating mammography, MRI, transcriptomic, and proteomic data to noninvasively predict axillary lymph node metastasis in breast cancer, achieving high accuracy across multiple validation cohorts. The model outperformed conventional ultrasound and used Grad-CAM to highlight tumor edges and surrounding tissue, suggesting clinical utility for guiding treatment decisions.

papers.title: Decoding promoter activity from DNA sequence using pre-trained language models.
papers.abstract: Promoter architecture plays a central role in transcriptional regulation, but predicting promoter activity directly from DNA sequence remains challenging. Here, we tested whether transformer-based DNA language models can learn regulatory logic encoded in Drosophila core promoters. We fine-tuned the pretrained DNA language model DNABERT-2 using a synthetic core promoter dataset measured in S2 cells with luciferase reporter assays. The model predicted promoter activity well when biological replicates were split between training and test data (R² ≈ 0.91), and retained meaningful performance when
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that a pre-trained DNA language model (DNABERT-2) can predict promoter activity from DNA sequence in Drosophila, achieving high accuracy when training and test data share biological replicates. The model's performance drops when tested on held-out sequences, highlighting the challenge of generalizing across different promoter contexts. The work suggests that transformer-based models can learn regulatory logic from core promoter sequences, but careful experimental design is needed to assess true predictive power.

papers.title: EnzymeHunter: Achieving fine-grained enzyme function prediction with a hierarchically aware contrastive learning framework.
papers.abstract: Accurate enzyme function annotation is a grand challenge due to the vast number of uncharacterized proteins and the difficulty of distinguishing subtle functions. We introduce EnzymeHunter, a deep-learning framework that achieves fine-grained prediction via a hierarchically aware contrastive learning strategy. By integrating sequence and structural information and using the Enzyme Commission (EC) hierarchy to guide its loss function, our model learns a functionally coherent embedding space where distances reflect precise levels of catalytic similarity. EnzymeHunter significantly outperforms st
papers.summary: EnzymeHunter is a new deep-learning tool that predicts enzyme functions with high precision by using a contrastive learning strategy that respects the hierarchical Enzyme Commission classification system. It integrates both sequence and structural data to learn an embedding space where distances between enzymes reflect their catalytic similarity. The model significantly outperforms existing methods, offering a promising approach for annotating the vast number of uncharacterized proteins.

papers.title: H3BERTa: A CDR-H3-specific language model for antibody repertoire analysis.
papers.abstract: Antibodies are central to immune defense and therapeutic design, yet predicting functional sequences remains challenging. Deep learning models trained on full variable regions often struggle due to sparse experimental data, signal dilution from conserved framework residues, and extreme diversity of hypervariable loops. The heavy-chain complementarity-determining region 3 (CDR-H3) is the most variable segment, shaping antigen specificity and immune diversity. Here, we present H3BERTa, a language model trained solely on CDR-H3 sequences to assess the extent of biological information encoded by t
papers.summary: H3BERTa is a new language model trained exclusively on CDR-H3 sequences, the most diverse part of antibodies, to better predict functional antibody sequences. By focusing only on this hypervariable region, the model avoids signal dilution from conserved framework regions and captures biological information relevant to antigen specificity and immune diversity. This approach offers a more targeted tool for antibody repertoire analysis and therapeutic design.

papers.title: Enhancing tumor T cell antigen prediction by integrating deep protein representations.
papers.abstract: Cancer is a major threat to human health, and cancer immunotherapy is considered the most promising treatment option. It offers high effectiveness and precision, with fewer side effects than traditional treatments. Tumor T cell antigens (TTCA) are the proteins or protein fragments that stand on the surface of cancer cells and that are recognized by the immune system. However, physical experimental approaches to infer them might be costly and time-consuming, even though they are important for cancer immunotherapy.
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational method that improves the prediction of tumor T cell antigens by integrating deep protein representations. The approach aims to identify which proteins on cancer cells can be recognized by the immune system, which is crucial for developing effective immunotherapies. By using deep learning on protein sequences, the method offers a faster and cheaper alternative to experimental approaches.

papers.title: InstaNovo-P: a de novo peptide sequencing model for phosphoproteomics.
papers.abstract: Phosphorylation, a crucial post-translational modification (PTM), plays a central role in cellular signaling and disease mechanisms. Mass spectrometry-based phosphoproteomics is widely used for system-wide characterization of phosphorylation events. However, traditional methods struggle with accurate phosphorylated site localization, complex search spaces, and detecting sequences outside the reference database. Advances in de novo peptide sequencing offer opportunities to address these limitations, but have yet to become integrated and adapted for phosphoproteomics datasets. Here, we present I
papers.summary: InstaNovo-P is a new deep learning model designed specifically for de novo sequencing of phosphorylated peptides from mass spectrometry data. It addresses key limitations in phosphoproteomics, such as accurate localization of phosphorylation sites and detection of sequences not in reference databases, offering improved analysis of cellular signaling.

papers.title: A Physics-Informed Dual-Branch LSTM Network for UAV Position and Attitude Estimation.
papers.abstract: To mitigate error accumulation and long-term drift in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) position and attitude estimation using purely inertial measurement unit (IMU) data, this paper presents a dual-branch physics-informed long short-term memory (DPI-LSTM) network incorporating shared temporal encoding, a dual-branch structured regression framework, and physical consistency constraints. The model employs a long short-term memory (LSTM)-based temporal encoder to extract temporal features from IMU time-window sequences. Established inertial kinematic relationships are embedded into the dual-branch L
papers.summary: This paper presents a deep learning model that combines LSTM networks with physical laws to improve UAV position and attitude estimation from IMU data, reducing error accumulation and drift. The dual-branch architecture with physics-informed constraints outperforms purely data-driven approaches.

papers.title: Machine Learning and Deep Learning Frameworks for Human-Virus Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction: Emerging Architectures, Methods, Benchmarks, and Challenges.
papers.abstract: The outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), has emerged as one of the most significant global health crises in recent history. Coronaviruses are a diverse group of RNA viruses classified into alpha, beta, gamma, and delta genera, with SARS-CoV-2 belonging to the beta-coronavirus family. The virus exhibits high transmissibility and causes a wide spectrum of clinical manifestations ranging from mild respiratory symptoms to severe complications such as acute respiratory distress syndrome, multi-organ failure, and dea
papers.summary: This review surveys machine learning and deep learning methods for predicting interactions between human and viral proteins, with a focus on architectures, benchmarks, and challenges highlighted by the COVID-19 pandemic. It provides a comprehensive overview of emerging computational approaches that can accelerate the identification of virus-host interactions for therapeutic development.

papers.title: L-Proline-Mediated Modulation of Astringency in Black Chokeberry Puree: Molecular Interactions, Process Optimization, and Quality Preservation.
papers.abstract: Aronia melanocarpa puree is rich in anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins, but its pronounced tannin-derived astringency limits product acceptance. This study developed a non-removal astringency-modulation strategy using food-grade L-proline and evaluated its molecular basis, processing window, and quality effects. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic analyses suggested that L-proline altered the local microenvironment of procyanidin B2 (PC-B2) through hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic association, and molecular packing rearrangement, without evident disru
papers.summary: This study developed a method to reduce the astringency of black chokeberry puree by adding L-proline, which interacts with tannins through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic associations without removing them. The approach preserves the fruit's beneficial anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins while improving taste, offering a food-grade solution for product acceptance.

papers.title: A Fast Demodulation Algorithm for Fibre Bragg Grating Based on the TimeMixer-LightGBM Hybrid Learning Framework.
papers.abstract: Fibre Bragg Grating (FBG) demodulation technology is central to structural health monitoring. However, spectral distortion and noise caused by complex environments, along with the challenge of balancing accuracy and real-time performance in existing deep learning algorithms, severely limit its application in large-scale dynamic sensing networks. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a hybrid learning framework named TimeMixer-LightGBM. The framework first employs a pure MLP-based TimeMixer model to efficiently extract multi-scale spectral sequence features from FBG reflection spectra,
papers.summary: This paper presents a hybrid machine learning framework combining TimeMixer and LightGBM for faster and more accurate demodulation of Fiber Bragg Grating sensors, addressing the trade-off between precision and real-time performance in structural health monitoring. The approach uses a multi-layer perceptron-based model to extract spectral features efficiently, enabling application in large-scale dynamic sensing networks.

papers.title: scDifformer: diffusion-based post-training for virtual cell modeling across large-scale single-cell data.
papers.abstract: Virtual cells represent a promising paradigm to understand cellular mechanisms, behavior, and dynamics. The realization of virtual cells relies on the accurate modeling of cellular dynamics from large-scale, multi-modal single-cell data. However, experiment-specific technical noise and intrinsic biological heterogeneity pose major challenges for virtual cell modeling. To address this gap, we present scDifformer, a context-aware transformer model augmented with a denoising diffusion module and a dedicated post-training phase. This three-phase design, comprising masked language model pre-trainin
papers.summary: scDifformer is a new computational method that combines a transformer model with a denoising diffusion module to create more accurate 'virtual cells' from large-scale single-cell data. It addresses challenges from technical noise and biological heterogeneity by using a three-phase design of pre-training, diffusion-based post-training, and context-aware modeling. This approach improves the ability to simulate cellular dynamics and understand cell behavior.

papers.title: AbTune: layer-wise selective fine-tuning of protein language models for antibodies.
papers.abstract: Antibodies play central roles in immune defense and are widely used as therapeutic agents. However, the high structural and sequence diversity of antigen-binding loops, combined with limited experimental data and weak co-evolutionary signals, makes it difficult to develop generalizable predictive models. In this work, we investigate test-time fine-tuning strategies to improve protein language model (pLM) performance in low-data settings, with a focus on antibody-related tasks. Systematic evaluations across tasks show that carefully constrained fine-tuning greatly enhances performance while pre
papers.summary: This paper introduces AbTune, a method for selectively fine-tuning protein language models on antibody tasks, which improves performance in low-data settings by carefully constraining which layers are updated. The approach addresses the challenge of modeling diverse antibody loops with limited experimental data.

papers.title: Advancing bioinformatics with language models: components, applications, and perspectives.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are deep learning-based artificial intelligence models that have achieved remarkable success in natural language processing. Typically composed of neural networks with billions of parameters, they are trained on massive unlabeled datasets using self-supervised or semi-supervised learning. Beyond language, LLMs hold immense potential for addressing complex bioinformatics challenges. This review provides a comprehensive overview of transformer-based model applications in genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. We discuss criti
papers.summary: This review provides a comprehensive overview of how large language models (LLMs) can be applied to bioinformatics challenges, covering their use in genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. It discusses the components of transformer-based models and their potential to transform biological data analysis.

papers.title: RD-OMICS: An Integrative Multi-Omics Data Inventory in Rare Diseases.
papers.abstract: Rare diseases (RD) impact over 30 million individuals in the United States, yet fewer than 5% of the identified conditions have FDA-approved treatments. Progress in RD research is hindered by small patient cohorts, biological heterogeneity, and the fragmented, inconsistently annotated publicly available omics data, which limits integrative analysis and translational discovery. Here, we present RD-OMICS, a data inventory with integrated and structured RD omics data from Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO), in the form of a knowledge graph. We developed a metadata harmonization pipeline that combines
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: RD-OMICS is a new knowledge graph that integrates and harmonizes rare disease omics data from public repositories, addressing the fragmentation and inconsistent annotation that hinders research. This resource aims to enable integrative analysis and translational discovery for rare diseases, where fewer than 5% have FDA-approved treatments.

papers.title: Accelerometry-Based Assessment of Overnight Coat Use on Dog Sleep and Activity Patterns: Implications for Farm Dog Welfare.
papers.abstract: New Zealand working farm dogs can struggle to maintain their body weight and condition during periods of high workload, despite substantial energy intake, highlighting the need to optimise energy balance. One potential strategy for reducing overall energy demands is to reduce energy expenditure for thermoregulation, particularly during colder conditions, which may improve both recovery and energy utilisation. This study investigated whether wearing coats influenced the sleep behaviour, activity, and apparent nutrient digestibility of outdoor-kennelled dogs. Eight adult working-breed dogs (n =
papers.journal: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI
papers.summary: This study tested whether having outdoor-kennelled farm dogs wear coats at night affects their sleep, activity, and nutrient digestion. The results showed that coat use did not significantly change sleep behavior, activity levels, or apparent nutrient digestibility in the dogs. The findings suggest that providing coats may not be an effective strategy for improving energy balance or welfare in working farm dogs under these conditions.

papers.title: A Multi-Engine Consensus Docking Pipeline for RNA Aptamer Screening Against ACC Oxidase (ACO): Statistical Validation, Machine Learning Analysis, and Pilot Cross-Target Evaluation Against ACC Synthase (ACS).
papers.abstract: Postharvest losses in climacteric fruits are largely driven by ethylene, and inhibiting its biosynthesis is an active research goal. RNA aptamers are attractive candidates for modulating the two rate-limiting enzymes-ACC oxidase (ACO) and ACC synthase (ACS), without the toxicity concerns of chemical inhibitors. We built a computational pipeline using three independent docking engines: ITD DOCK (Graphics Processing Unit [GPU]-accelerated, Assisted Model Building with Energy Refinement [AMBER] force fields), HDOCK Lite (knowledge-based geometric potentials), and HADDOCK3 (semi-flexible refinemen
papers.summary: This study presents a computational pipeline that combines three different docking engines to screen RNA aptamers against ACC oxidase, a key enzyme in ethylene biosynthesis. The pipeline was validated statistically and with machine learning, and showed potential for cross-target application against ACC synthase. This approach could aid in developing RNA-based inhibitors to reduce postharvest fruit losses without the toxicity of chemical inhibitors.

papers.title: Affinage: genome-scale mechanistic gene annotation from the published literature.
papers.abstract: Understanding the mechanistic function of a gene is a critical starting point for biology. However, for much of the human proteome that knowledge is scattered across thousands of primary papers or remains poorly established, while the curated databases biologists rely on can lag years behind recent literature. Large language models can now read and synthesize that literature on demand, but doing so faithfully for many genes is an expensive, non-reproducible retrieval session that does not scale across users. Here, we present Affinage, an LLM pipeline that performs this retrieval and mechanisti
papers.summary: Affinage is a new LLM-based pipeline that automatically reads and synthesizes the published literature to produce mechanistic gene annotations at genome scale, addressing the problem that curated databases often lag behind recent findings. It aims to provide faithful, reproducible, and scalable retrieval of gene function knowledge that is currently scattered across thousands of papers. This approach could significantly accelerate biological discovery by making up-to-date mechanistic annotations accessible for the entire human proteome.

papers.title: Development of an interpretable machine learning model for predicting intestinal necrosis in older patients with incarcerated hernia: A retrospective cohort study.
papers.abstract: ObjectiveTo develop a machine learning model for predicting intestinal necrosis in older patients with incarcerated hernia and interpret the model using SHapley additive exPlanations (SHAP) to assist clinicians in early identification of intestinal necrosis and formulating more appropriate treatment strategies.MethodsThis retrospective cohort study analyzed 324 older patients with incarcerated hernia who were admitted to the Department of Emergency Surgery and Department of Hernia and Abdominal Wall Surgery at the Affiliated Lu'an Hospital of Anhui Medical University between March 2015 and Feb
papers.journal: The Journal of international medical research
papers.summary: This study developed a machine learning model to predict intestinal necrosis in older patients with incarcerated hernia, using SHAP to make the model interpretable for clinicians. The model aims to help doctors identify intestinal necrosis early and choose better treatment strategies.

papers.title: Deep learning-driven discovery and mechanism of action study of a minimalist conopeptide targeting α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor.
papers.abstract: Despite extensive structural and functional characterization of the α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, valuable structural insights into its interactions with conopeptides remain limited, thereby hindering the rational development of peptide-based modulators for this clinically important receptor subtype. Here, we present an integrated pipeline combining deep learning, structural biology, computational modeling and electrophysiology to accelerate the discovery and optimization of α7 nAChR-targeting conopeptides. To overcome data scarcity, we developed a deep learning model using the ESM-2 pr
papers.summary: Researchers developed a deep learning pipeline integrating ESM-2, structural biology, and electrophysiology to discover and characterize a minimalist conopeptide that targets the α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, overcoming data scarcity to identify peptide-based modulators for this clinically important receptor.

papers.title: FlowDock: A unified flow-based framework for flexible protein-ligand docking and binding affinity prediction.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand complexes and binding affinity is critical for hit identification and optimization for structure-based drug design. Traditional docking simulates binding processes with searching algorithms guided by energy-scoring functions, which are quite computationally expensive and time-intensive. In contrast, deep learning approaches offer a cost-effective alternative, yet often generate conformations with limited physicochemical validity and fail to account for protein flexibility. To address these pitfalls, we propose FlowDock, a multitask framework enhanced by Ba
papers.summary: FlowDock is a new deep learning framework that simultaneously predicts protein-ligand docking poses and binding affinity, addressing key limitations of traditional methods by accounting for protein flexibility and generating physically valid conformations. It offers a computationally efficient alternative to traditional docking while improving accuracy and physicochemical realism.

papers.title: Accurate and task-agnostic modeling of enzymatic reactions through multimodal relational learning.
papers.abstract: Enzymatic reactions play an emerging role in a broad spectrum of scientific and industrial applications. The inherent complexity of enzymes, such as their substrate specificity, conformational flexibility, and the vast diversity of reactions involved, poses substantial challenges for the advanced computational prediction of enzymatic reactions with desirable accuracy. Moreover, existing approaches are mostly tailored for a specific sub-task, such as substrate prediction or binding site annotation, which limits their applicability. In this study, we introduce ERAM, a task-agnostic multimodal le
papers.summary: The authors developed ERAM, a multimodal relational learning model that can accurately predict various aspects of enzymatic reactions without being limited to a single sub-task like substrate prediction or binding site annotation. By integrating different data types, the model overcomes challenges related to enzyme complexity and reaction diversity, offering a versatile tool for computational enzymology.

papers.title: Unveiling the bioactive landscape of drug inactive ingredients (DIGs) using deep transfer learning.
papers.abstract: In a drug product, the major components by mass are the drug inactive ingredients (DIGs), which raises great concerns about their unwanted effects and clinical toxicities. It is demanded to unveil their proteome-wide bioactive landscape using computational methods. However, existing methods are impeded by either incapability to scan human proteome or inaccuracy in DIGs' bioactivity prediction. Here, a cross-attention transformer model, titled TransDIG, leveraging cross-module deep transfer learning was therefore developed to map the bioactive landscape of DIGs using minimal experimental data.
papers.summary: Researchers developed TransDIG, a cross-attention transformer model using deep transfer learning, to predict the proteome-wide bioactivity of drug inactive ingredients (DIGs). This addresses the need to assess potential unwanted effects and toxicities of DIGs, which are major components of drug products by mass. The model overcomes limitations of existing methods by enabling accurate scanning of the entire human proteome with minimal experimental data.

papers.title: Reaction-aware molecular representation learning: Toward generalizable artificial intelligence for enzymatic catalysis.
papers.summary: This paper introduces a reaction-aware molecular representation learning method that improves AI's ability to predict enzymatic catalysis by incorporating reaction context into molecular embeddings. The approach enhances generalizability across different enzyme families and reaction types, potentially accelerating drug discovery and metabolic engineering.

papers.title: Comparative Evaluation of Large Language Models for Reporting Jaw Lesions on Panoramic Radiographs.
papers.abstract: Background/Objectives: The aim of this study was to assess the diagnostic capabilities of three large language model-based artificial intelligence chatbots (ChatGPT 4.0, Gemini 2.5, and Microsoft Copilot) in the radiographic evaluation of jaw lesions on panoramic images with different densities (mixed, radiolucent, and radiopaque). Methods: 120 panoramic radiographs showing jaw lesions with varying radiographic appearances were independently analyzed using three artificial intelligence chatbot systems. Each model was provided with the same single-round prompt and a standardized diagnostic scor
papers.summary: This study evaluated three large language model-based AI chatbots (ChatGPT 4.0, Gemini 2.5, and Microsoft Copilot) for their ability to diagnose jaw lesions from panoramic radiographs. The models were tested on 120 images with varying lesion densities, showing that LLMs have potential as diagnostic aids in dental radiology but require further refinement for clinical use.

papers.title: Learning millisecond protein dynamics from what is missing in NMR spectra.
papers.abstract: Many proteins' biological functions rely on interconversions between multiple conformations occurring at micro-to millisecond (µs-ms) timescales. A lack of standardized, large-scale experimental data has hindered obtaining a more predictive understanding of these motions. After curating >100 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) relaxation datasets, we realized an observable for µs-ms dynamics might be hiding in plain sight. Millisecond dynamics can cause NMR signals to broaden beyond detection, leaving some residues not assigned in the chemical shift datasets of ~10,000 proteins deposited in the B
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study reveals that unassigned residues in NMR chemical shift datasets, often considered missing data, actually encode information about millisecond-scale protein dynamics. By curating over 100 NMR relaxation datasets and analyzing ~10,000 deposited protein datasets, the authors show that these 'missing' signals can serve as a large-scale experimental observable for micro-to-millisecond conformational changes. This finding provides a new, standardized approach to study functionally important protein motions that were previously difficult to capture systematically.

papers.title: Linguistics and human brain: a perspective of computational neuroscience.
papers.abstract: Elucidating the language-brain relationship requires bridging the methodological gap between linguistics' abstract theoretical frameworks and neuroscience's empirical neural data. As an interdisciplinary cornerstone, computational neuroscience formalizes language's hierarchical and dynamic structures into testable neural representation models through modeling, simulation, and data analysis, enabling computational dialogue between linguistic hypotheses and neural mechanisms. Recent advances in deep learning, particularly large language models (LLMs), have further advanced this inquiry: their hi
papers.summary: This perspective paper discusses how computational neuroscience bridges linguistics and neuroscience by formalizing language structures into testable neural models. It highlights recent advances in deep learning, especially large language models (LLMs), as powerful tools for exploring the relationship between language and brain function.

papers.title: Developing a prediction model for poor prognosis in MPA patients using initial admission examination results: a machine learning study from Southwest China.
papers.abstract: Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) is one of the main types of ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV), but current admission examination indicators are limited in predicting poor prognosis for MPA. This study aims to develop a prediction model for adverse prognosis in MPA patients using initial admission examination results.
papers.summary: This study developed a machine learning model using initial hospital admission test results to predict poor outcomes in patients with microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), a type of ANCA-associated vasculitis. The model aims to improve prognostic accuracy beyond current limited indicators, using data from patients in Southwest China.

papers.title: Recent applications of artificial intelligence in cancer radiotherapy and immunotherapy: current status and future directions.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) enhances the precision, personalization, and efficiency of cancer treatment through deep learning and machine learning techniques. This review comprehensively examines the evolution of AI and its expanding applications in cancer radiotherapy, immunotherapy, and drug discovery and repurposing. In radiotherapy, AI enables automated medical image segmentation, thereby facilitating the accurate delineation of tumor targets. Furthermore, AI-driven feedback systems support clinicians in developing individualized treatment plans by offering real-time assessment of treatme
papers.summary: This review article discusses how artificial intelligence, particularly deep learning and machine learning, is improving cancer radiotherapy and immunotherapy by enabling automated tumor segmentation, personalized treatment planning, and real-time treatment assessment. It also covers AI applications in drug discovery and repurposing for cancer therapy. The paper provides a comprehensive overview of current AI tools and future directions in oncology.

papers.title: Deep learning in multi-omics integration for gastrointestinal cancer biomarker discovery.
papers.abstract: Gastrointestinal (GI) cancers remain leading causes of cancer-related morbidity and mortality worldwide, driven by complex molecular mechanisms involving genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, and radiomic alterations. The discovery of reliable biomarkers is essential for improving early detection, risk stratification, prognosis prediction, and personalized treatment strategies. Recent advances in deep learning (DL) have enabled the integration of multi-omics and imaging data, offering unprecedented opportunities to identify clinically relevant biomarkers in these malignancies. This
papers.summary: This review discusses how deep learning methods can integrate diverse data types (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and radiomics) to discover biomarkers for gastrointestinal cancers. The authors highlight that these computational approaches offer new opportunities for improving early detection, prognosis, and personalized treatment by uncovering clinically relevant molecular patterns.

papers.title: Tumor microenvironment-oriented HNGCIscore identifies immunotherapy-sensitive subgroups in ICI retreatment of HER2-negative advanced gastric cancer.
papers.abstract: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor-based chemoimmunotherapy has become a standard first-line treatment for HER2-negative advanced gastric or gastro-esophageal junction cancer. However, most patients eventually experience disease progression, and optimal post-progression strategies, particularly immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI)-based retreatment, remain unclear. This study evaluated the real-world feasibility of ICI-based retreatment and developed an exploratory tumor microenvironment (TME)-oriented biomarker framework to stratify patients who may benefit from immunotherapy.
papers.summary: This study explores the use of immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI)-based retreatment for HER2-negative advanced gastric cancer patients who progressed after initial chemoimmunotherapy. The authors developed a tumor microenvironment-oriented biomarker score (HNGCIscore) that can identify patient subgroups more likely to benefit from ICI retreatment, offering a potential strategy for personalizing post-progression therapy.

papers.title: Predicting ankylosing spondylitis disease activity via patient-reported outcome measures: Building prediction models based on machine learning.
papers.abstract: Disease activity is a critical indicator for monitoring the progression of ankylosing spondylitis (AS), guiding clinical decision-making, and informing treatment plans. Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) have gained prominence in AS clinical management. However, their potential to predict Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Score-C-reactive protein (ASDAS-CRP) remains unexplored. This study employs machine learning (ML) techniques to develop prediction models utilizing PROMs data to estimate disease activity in patients with AS.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict disease activity in ankylosing spondylitis from patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs), finding that PROMs can effectively estimate ASDAS-CRP scores. The models offer a non-invasive, patient-centered approach to monitoring disease progression. This could reduce reliance on blood tests for clinical decision-making.

papers.title: A modular semantic-structural pipeline for visual decoding from primate spiking data via selective temporal integration.
papers.abstract: Characterizing the information content of intracortical signals during visual processing is a central challenge in systems neuroscience. We address the problem of decoding visual information from high-density intracortical recordings in primates, using the THINGS Ventral Stream Spiking Dataset. We systematically evaluate the effects of model architecture, training objectives, and data scaling on decoding performance. Results show that decoding accuracy is jointly driven by non-linearity and selective temporal aggregation, rather than heavier sequence modelling in this data regime. A simple mod
papers.summary: This study systematically evaluates how to decode visual information from high-density intracortical recordings in primates, finding that decoding accuracy is driven by non-linearity and selective temporal aggregation rather than heavy sequence modeling. The results provide insights into the information content of neural signals during visual processing.

papers.title: A cluster analysis of 466 patients demonstrates that glutathione supplementation could preferentially benefit advanced unstable cirrhosis phenotype rather than stable cirrhosis.
papers.abstract: Chronic liver disease is characterized by progressive hepatic glutathione depletion and oxidative stress, yet antioxidant therapies have historically shown disappointing clinical efficacy in unselected populations. This therapeutic paradox may reflect inadequate patient stratification and failure to distinguish between acute reversible oxidative injury and chronic persistent inflammation. We hypothesized that glutathione supplementation may preferentially benefit distinct cirrhosis phenotypes with active systemic inflammation.
papers.summary: This study analyzed 466 cirrhosis patients and found that glutathione supplementation may be more beneficial for those with an advanced, unstable form of the disease characterized by active systemic inflammation, rather than for patients with stable cirrhosis. The findings suggest that previous clinical trials of antioxidants may have failed because they did not distinguish between different patient subgroups.

papers.title: Automated segmentation of pituitary adenomas, pituitary gland, and internal carotid arteries on routine coronal contrast-enhanced T1-weighted MRI: a single-sequence feasibility study.
papers.abstract: Pituitary adenomas (PAs) alter the anatomy of the pituitary gland (PG) and internal carotid arteries (ICAs), requiring accurate segmentation for surgical planning and risk assessment. Multi-sequence magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-based automatic segmentation methods have been employed but remain limited in routine clinical practice due to protocol inconsistencies. This study aimed to evaluate the feasibility and clinical relevance of Swin-Unet and nnU-Net for automated segmentation of the PA, PG, and ICA using only contrast-enhanced T1-weighted (T1CE) coronal MRI.
papers.summary: This study shows that deep learning models (Swin-Unet and nnU-Net) can accurately segment pituitary adenomas, the pituitary gland, and internal carotid arteries using just a single routine MRI sequence (contrast-enhanced T1-weighted coronal images), rather than requiring multiple sequences. This approach could make automated segmentation more practical for clinical use by reducing dependency on protocol consistency.

papers.title: AI-driven drug discovery using transformer-based molecular representation learning.
papers.abstract: The vast majority of chemically plausible, drug-like molecules remain unexplored due to the combinatorial scale of chemical space and the limited throughput of experimental screening. This is complicated by the lack of data and the inability to extrapolate predictive models to chemotypes that are not represented well. We introduce a transformer-based molecular modeling framework for target-specific potency prediction, trained on curated BindingDB bioactivity data across Alzheimer's, diabetes, and cancer targets to deliver accurate pIC50 regression and binary activity classification. It uses cu
papers.summary: This paper presents a transformer-based AI model for predicting drug potency against specific targets, trained on bioactivity data for Alzheimer's, diabetes, and cancer. The model achieves accurate predictions of pIC50 values and binary activity classification, addressing the challenge of exploring vast chemical space beyond known compounds.

papers.title: Historical, taxonomic, and cultural patterns in scientific naming across Animalia.
papers.abstract: Animal naming is fundamental to biological classification and scientific communication. Beyond their taxonomic function, scientific names may also reflect historical and cultural contexts. Owing to this dual role, species names have recently attracted increasing attention from historical and humanities perspectives for both their informative value and the broader patterns they may encode. To examine large-scale patterns in naming practices, we investigated temporal trends of scientific names across Animalia using automated large-language-model-based classification approaches. Our analyses reve
papers.summary: This study uses large language models to analyze historical and cultural patterns in the scientific naming of animal species across the entire Animalia kingdom. The authors find that naming practices reflect broader historical and cultural contexts beyond pure taxonomy. The work demonstrates the value of automated text analysis for uncovering large-scale trends in biological nomenclature.

papers.title: SLECA: A Single-Cell Atlas of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Enabling Rare-Cell Discovery Using Graph Transformer.
papers.abstract: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex autoimmune disease with multisystem involvement and marked interpatient heterogeneity. This heterogeneity has hindered precise characterization of the immune dysregulatory mechanisms underlying the disease. While rare immune cell populations are increasingly recognized as critical drivers of disease pathogenesis and progression, the lack of sufficiently powered, comprehensive single-cell transcriptomic resources has limited their systematic identification and characterization. To address this gap, we present SLECA, the first large-scale single-ce
papers.summary: SLECA is a large-scale single-cell atlas of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) that uses a graph transformer model to identify rare immune cell populations, which are often missed in smaller studies. This resource helps uncover key immune dysregulation mechanisms in SLE, addressing the challenge of patient heterogeneity.

papers.title: T-pGNN4DTI: Towards better drug-target interactions prediction using Global Self-attentive Pooled Graph Convolutional Networks and protein pre-training Models.
papers.abstract: Identification of drug-target interactions (DTI) is an important and challenging task in drug discovery and development. Traditional methods generally require biological experiments, which are costly and time-consuming. Machine learning-based methods can rapidly predict DTI using only computer algorithmic models, allowing researchers to validate only the most promising interactions through biochemical experiments. This holds promise for effectively addressing the current challenges of lengthy development cycles and high costs in new drug development. However, it is difficult for the existing D
papers.summary: This paper presents T-pGNN4DTI, a new machine learning method that combines graph neural networks with protein pre-training models to predict drug-target interactions more accurately. The approach uses a global self-attentive pooling mechanism to better capture important features from drug and protein structures, potentially reducing the need for costly lab experiments in drug discovery.

papers.title: PlantFormer: a precise plant disease segmentation network with interactive backbone and global-anisotropic context aggregation.
papers.abstract: Precise plant disease segmentation in real-world agricultural environments presents challenges that general-purpose models often fail to address, primarily due to the anisotropic spread of lesions, blurred biological boundaries, and severe background dominance. To overcome these bottlenecks, this paper proposes PlantFormer, an end-to-end network that effectively integrates and adapts advanced architectural components to address these domain-specific issues. Specifically, PlantFormer employs an InteractSwin Backbone with a Cross-Level Fusion (CLF) module to preserve early pathological details.
papers.summary: PlantFormer is a new deep learning network designed to accurately segment plant diseases in real-world images, overcoming challenges like irregular lesion shapes and blurred boundaries. It uses an interactive backbone and a cross-level fusion module to preserve fine pathological details, outperforming general-purpose models. This work provides a specialized tool for precision agriculture and plant disease monitoring.

papers.title: GraphTransDTI: A novel hybrid framework combining graph transformer and CNN-BiLSTM for enhanced Drug-Protein Interaction prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-protein interaction (DTI) prediction is a pivotal step in the drug discovery and repurposing process, helping to minimize experimental costs and time. However, existing deep learning methods often face limitations in simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the deep contextual correlation with protein sequences. To address this issue, we propose GraphTransDTI, a synergistic hybrid framework that integrates a Graph Transformer to represent drug graph structures, a CNN-BiLSTM network to encode protein sequence context, and a Cross-Attention mechanism to model cro
papers.summary: GraphTransDTI is a new deep learning framework that combines a Graph Transformer for drug molecular structures with a CNN-BiLSTM network for protein sequences, linked by a Cross-Attention mechanism to better predict drug-protein interactions. The method aims to overcome limitations of existing approaches in capturing both spatial drug features and deep protein sequence context simultaneously.
